channel.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest
channel.description: Your email newsletters, read to you. Every weekday this show gathers the newsletters from your inbox, weaves them into a single flowing narration, and delivers one easy-to-listen digest of the day's stories — so you can catch up on the go instead of scrolling through email.
channel.itunes:author: Newsletter Podcast Agent
channel.itunes:summary: Your email newsletters, read to you. Every weekday this show gathers the newsletters from your inbox, weaves them into a single flowing narration, and delivers one easy-to-listen digest of the day's stories — so you can catch up on the go instead of scrolling through email.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Wednesday, July 1, 2026
item.description: Today's episode covers Anthropic's new efficiency-focused Sonnet five model and the return of its export-blocked Fable five and Mythos five models, plus Google's two new creative AI tools, Nano Banana two Lite and Gemini Omni Flash, amid backlash over its A24 film partnership. It also digs into the industry-wide push into scientific research, led by Anthropic's new Claude Science workbench, and closes with quick hits on OpenAI's compute breakthrough, chipmaker Etched's funding, AWS's engineering investment, and a listener's marathon-training success using ChatGPT.
item.itunes:summary: Today's episode covers Anthropic's new efficiency-focused Sonnet five model and the return of its export-blocked Fable five and Mythos five models, plus Google's two new creative AI tools, Nano Banana two Lite and Gemini Omni Flash, amid backlash over its A24 film partnership. It also digs into the industry-wide push into scientific research, led by Anthropic's new Claude Science workbench, and closes with quick hits on OpenAI's compute breakthrough, chipmaker Etched's funding, AWS's engineering investment, and a listener's marathon-training success using ChatGPT.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Tuesday, June 30, 2026
item.description: Today's episode explores the practical realities of deploying AI, starting with Ford rehiring 350 veteran engineers after over-relying on automation, and the broader case for augmentation over replacement. It also covers Qualcomm's push into AI data centers with its Dragonfly brand and Modular acquisition that could challenge Nvidia's CUDA dominance, Anthropic's discounted Claude deal with the state of California, and Meta's non-invasive Brain2Qwerty system that decodes typed sentences from brain scans. Rounding it out are quick hits on Cursor's new mobile app, Anthropic's Economic Index, an Apple exec's move to OpenAI, South Korea's chip investment plan, and more.
item.itunes:summary: Today's episode explores the practical realities of deploying AI, starting with Ford rehiring 350 veteran engineers after over-relying on automation, and the broader case for augmentation over replacement. It also covers Qualcomm's push into AI data centers with its Dragonfly brand and Modular acquisition that could challenge Nvidia's CUDA dominance, Anthropic's discounted Claude deal with the state of California, and Meta's non-invasive Brain2Qwerty system that decodes typed sentences from brain scans. Rounding it out are quick hits on Cursor's new mobile app, Anthropic's Economic Index, an Apple exec's move to OpenAI, South Korea's chip investment plan, and more.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Monday, June 29, 2026
item.description: Today's episode centers on OpenAI's release of GPT-5.6, a three-tier model family (Sol, Terra, and Luna) that tops key benchmarks but remains restricted to a handful of government-vetted partners, alongside shifting frontier competition from Anthropic, Grok, and Chinese labs. It also covers Google Research's push to use AI for scientific discovery, a study showing most workplaces lack AI guidance and training, and new figures on the staggering economics of the generative AI industry, including revenue growth, token usage, and surging electricity demand. Rounding it out are practical AI use cases, compute capacity squeezes, and quick hits on Codex, OpenAI hires, and new funding.
item.itunes:summary: Today's episode centers on OpenAI's release of GPT-5.6, a three-tier model family (Sol, Terra, and Luna) that tops key benchmarks but remains restricted to a handful of government-vetted partners, alongside shifting frontier competition from Anthropic, Grok, and Chinese labs. It also covers Google Research's push to use AI for scientific discovery, a study showing most workplaces lack AI guidance and training, and new figures on the staggering economics of the generative AI industry, including revenue growth, token usage, and surging electricity demand. Rounding it out are practical AI use cases, compute capacity squeezes, and quick hits on Codex, OpenAI hires, and new funding.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Saturday, June 27, 2026
item.description: This episode focuses on the security risks of autonomous AI coding agents like Claude Code, explaining the "lethal trifecta" of internet access, broad permissions, and sensitive code that makes running them on personal laptops dangerous. Drawing on a special edition of The Deep View in partnership with Coder, it covers why IT teams block these tools, the irony that those who'd benefit most can't get access, and a proposed fix: moving agents to pre-hardened, centrally governed remote cloud environments. It closes with the bigger picture of running fleets of audited agents in parallel as a standard way of working.
item.itunes:summary: This episode focuses on the security risks of autonomous AI coding agents like Claude Code, explaining the "lethal trifecta" of internet access, broad permissions, and sensitive code that makes running them on personal laptops dangerous. Drawing on a special edition of The Deep View in partnership with Coder, it covers why IT teams block these tools, the irony that those who'd benefit most can't get access, and a proposed fix: moving agents to pre-hardened, centrally governed remote cloud environments. It closes with the bigger picture of running fleets of audited agents in parallel as a standard way of working.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Friday, June 26, 2026
item.description: This episode digs into the rise of AI agents and the guardrails forming around them, starting with OpenAI's new research showing agents like Codex overtaking chatbots in the workplace, followed by the $500 million bipartisan Raise Us initiative aimed at heading off AI-driven job disruption. It also covers a push to secure agents at the chip level from the Confidential Computing Summit, the Trump administration limiting the release of OpenAI's GPT-5.6, and Anthropic accusing Alibaba of a massive Claude data-harvesting attack. Quicker hits include Meta's Virtue AI hires, Apple price increases, new Excel Copilot tools, and a media founder's reflections on AI avatars and authenticity.
item.itunes:summary: This episode digs into the rise of AI agents and the guardrails forming around them, starting with OpenAI's new research showing agents like Codex overtaking chatbots in the workplace, followed by the $500 million bipartisan Raise Us initiative aimed at heading off AI-driven job disruption. It also covers a push to secure agents at the chip level from the Confidential Computing Summit, the Trump administration limiting the release of OpenAI's GPT-5.6, and Anthropic accusing Alibaba of a massive Claude data-harvesting attack. Quicker hits include Meta's Virtue AI hires, Apple price increases, new Excel Copilot tools, and a media founder's reflections on AI avatars and authenticity.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Thursday, June 25, 2026
item.description: OpenAI launches its first custom inference chip, Jalapeño, built with Broadcom, as the company pushes to own every layer of the AI stack. The episode also covers KPMG data on how workers are adopting AI agents, Figma's new "team sport" AI tools from its Config event, a $500 million nonprofit called Intercept aiming to end the common cold, and the latest developments in Anthropic's regulatory standoff with the government. Plus quick hits on AI talent moves, Google DeepMind's Gemini computer-use feature, a World Cup ticket-scouting tip, and a reader's Claude-built app for selective mutism.
item.itunes:summary: OpenAI launches its first custom inference chip, Jalapeño, built with Broadcom, as the company pushes to own every layer of the AI stack. The episode also covers KPMG data on how workers are adopting AI agents, Figma's new "team sport" AI tools from its Config event, a $500 million nonprofit called Intercept aiming to end the common cold, and the latest developments in Anthropic's regulatory standoff with the government. Plus quick hits on AI talent moves, Google DeepMind's Gemini computer-use feature, a World Cup ticket-scouting tip, and a reader's Claude-built app for selective mutism.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Wednesday, June 24, 2026
item.description: Today's episode centers on AI entering the workplace, leading with Anthropic's Claude Tag, which embeds Claude into Slack as a delegatable "team member," and a Confidential Computing Summit push for data decentralization and agent security. It also covers how AI is reshaping hiring and the skills employers now want, Meta's cheaper $299 smart glasses, Stanford's new Proto framework for combining AI biology models, and a rapid-fire roundup including Nvidia's BioNeMo toolkit, new OCR and voice models, and government scrutiny of Meta's AI.
item.itunes:summary: Today's episode centers on AI entering the workplace, leading with Anthropic's Claude Tag, which embeds Claude into Slack as a delegatable "team member," and a Confidential Computing Summit push for data decentralization and agent security. It also covers how AI is reshaping hiring and the skills employers now want, Meta's cheaper $299 smart glasses, Stanford's new Proto framework for combining AI biology models, and a rapid-fire roundup including Nvidia's BioNeMo toolkit, new OCR and voice models, and government scrutiny of Meta's AI.

item.title: My Daily Newsletter Digest — Monday, June 22, 2026
item.description: This episode covers the escalating standoff between Anthropic and the U.S. government over export controls on its top models, including a "Free Fable" letter from security researchers and tensions at the G7 AI summit. It also tracks Google DeepMind's loss of star researchers Noam Shazeer and John Jumper, dueling studies on whether AI augments or eliminates jobs, Snap's new true-AR Specs, and Midjourney's surprise full-body medical scanner and spa plans. Other stories include SpaceX's sixty-billion-dollar acquisition of Cursor, enterprise "token panic" over AI costs, Databricks' open-systems strategy, agent governance, China's rising AI standing, and shifting public sentiment toward the technology.
item.itunes:summary: This episode covers the escalating standoff between Anthropic and the U.S. government over export controls on its top models, including a "Free Fable" letter from security researchers and tensions at the G7 AI summit. It also tracks Google DeepMind's loss of star researchers Noam Shazeer and John Jumper, dueling studies on whether AI augments or eliminates jobs, Snap's new true-AR Specs, and Midjourney's surprise full-body medical scanner and spa plans. Other stories include SpaceX's sixty-billion-dollar acquisition of Cursor, enterprise "token panic" over AI costs, Databricks' open-systems strategy, agent governance, China's rising AI standing, and shifting public sentiment toward the technology.
